video_title: A.I. Mania Creating Twin Bubbles In Stock Prices AND Earnings | New Harbor Financial
gist: New Harbor Financial's Mike Preston and John Lloyd argue the AI rally is a 'twin bubble' in both valuations and earnings, driven by a vertical, ultra-narrow semiconductor melt-up they read as a late-stage blowoff top; they hold ~48% equity, hedge with options, and warn that at historic-extreme valuations the next decade likely delivers near-zero to negative real returns regardless of how real the AI buildout is.

insights.claim: New Harbor believes the AI complex is a 'twin bubble' — both an overvaluation bubble (multiples on inflated forward estimates) and an earnings bubble (the forward earnings themselves are too optimistic) — even though AI is genuinely transformative like the internet.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: If both the multiple AND the underlying earnings re-rate together, the downside in AI names compounds; investors should not assume 'real technology' protects valuations.
insights.quote: We probably have a valuation and an earnings bubble. Doesn't mean these aren't real. They're not transformative, just like the internet was. But we've gotten way ahead of ourselves.

insights.claim: Reported AI-related earnings quality is suspect because of third-party deals and circular financing, so a 'quality of earnings' study would likely reveal that the boom rests partly on non-organic accounting.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Circular vendor financing means a single counterparty pullback could cascade through reported revenue; watch for related-party disclosure in AI earnings.
insights.quote: a lot of these um you know thirdparty deals and and circular financing all this stuff

insights.claim: Large hyperscalers are beginning to rein in AI spend — Microsoft reportedly pulled back on using agentic AI for coding because it was spending too much without showing return on investment — signaling the capex narrative can flip quickly.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Hyperscaler capex is the demand floor under semis; early signs of spending discipline are a leading indicator that semi revenue estimates could be cut.
insights.quote: Microsoft um basically ska turn pulled in the reigns on their employee base using u agentic AI for coding you know because they're spending too much

insights.claim: The AI rally shows late-stage bubble behavior: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang merely naming Marvell as a potential next trillion-dollar company sent that stock up ~30% in a day.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Price moves driven by CEO soundbites rather than fundamentals are a classic blowoff-phase tell; treat such spikes as sentiment, not value.
insights.quote: the next trillion dollar company is going to be I think he said Marvel technology ... and the stock went up like 30% I think in a day

insights.claim: The semiconductor ETF SMH roughly tripled (from ~250-300 to over 600) in under two months, a near-vertical move New Harbor compares to the parabolic precious-metals run earlier in the year that then corrected.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston / John Lloyd (guests, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Vertical moves 'don't resolve by going sideways' — they tend to draw real pullbacks; SMH is the cleanest expression of the bubble risk to fade or hedge.
insights.quote: The ETF SMH semiconductor holders went from like 250 or 300 and it shot higher than 600 in a very short period of time

insights.claim: When AI narratives break, analysts reluctantly cut earnings forecasts AND compress multiples simultaneously (e.g. from 30x toward 15-20x), so the same stock gets hit twice — lower multiple on lower earnings.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Models the mechanics of a twin-bubble de-rating; the double hit explains why these unwinds are far larger than a simple multiple compression.
insights.quote: Maybe it's only worth 20 or maybe 15. Oh, and by the way ... the earnings themselves ... also is being cut.

insights.claim: 2026 YTD S&P 500 gains (~11%) are driven almost entirely by just info-tech (AI/semis) and energy, with financials (XLF stuck near 50) and healthcare/pharma (XLV) lagging — narrowness New Harbor flags as a warning sign.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Without laggard sectors broadening out, the rally is fragile; watch XLF and XLV for confirmation or for the leaders to roll over.
insights.quote: Really, it's just info and energy. That's it

insights.claim: Mike Preston believes this multi-decade bull cycle ends in a blowoff top rather than a normal correction, a view he only formed in the last year or two after every overvalued pullback was immediately bought in an increasingly vertical V-shaped recovery.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Frames the base case as one final parabolic surge then a major reset — not a buy-the-dip regime indefinitely; position for an eventual cycle top.
insights.quote: every single pullback, no matter how overvalued, got immediately bought in V-shaped recovery. in every V-shaped recovery was more vertical

insights.claim: Preston's concrete blowoff-top trigger is the S&P 500 gaining roughly 500 points / 7-10% within a few weeks from a genuine breakout (not a V-bottom), and he believes that signal already fired when the index broke 7,000 and ran to ~7,600 in about three weeks.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Gives a falsifiable marker that, by Preston's own framework, the terminal blowoff phase has likely begun; another ~1,000 S&P points is plausible if breadth expands.
insights.quote: we broke out of the old high at 7,000 went right to 7500 within like 3 weeks we're sitting at 7600 here

insights.claim: Major market tops typically 'fall apart from within' — indices make new highs while internal participation deteriorates and leaders abruptly roll over — so the current narrowness fits a late-top profile.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Breadth/participation indicators, not price level, are the better top-timing tools; deteriorating internals under new highs is the signal to de-risk.
insights.quote: if you look at major market tops, they usually start to fall apart from within

insights.claim: The S&P 500 has had 8 consecutive up weeks (~17% rally, 99th percentile) — historically rare (only ~20 times since 1950) — but Lloyd stresses these extremity readings are poor timing tools because forward returns after such streaks have averaged positive and streaks have run as long as 13 weeks.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Counters the naive 'overbought = sell' reflex; don't short merely on extension — wait for breadth/weight-of-evidence indicators to break.
insights.quote: Only 20 times have there been eight consecutive weeks up in the S&P 500

insights.claim: Markets reprice at the margin, not on aggregate flows — like one neighbor selling a house 20% below Zillow resets the whole neighborhood, a small shift from buyers being less eager than sellers can crack the market without any news catalyst.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Explains why tops occur 'on exhaustion' with no headline; don't wait for a trigger event to de-risk a stretched, narrow market.
insights.quote: It just takes on the margin buyers being somewhat less eager than sellers. That's really all it takes.

themes.theme: AI capex, the real economy & recession timing

insights.claim: US data center construction spending jumped 28% YoY in April to a record ~$50B annualized, surpassing public transportation construction spending (~$49.9B) for the first time in history.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: Quantifies how AI buildout is now a macro-scale demand source; this single line item is propping up GDP and delaying recession.
insights.quote: data center construction spending has outpaced government transportation spending for the first time in history

insights.claim: Taggart argues a US recession is hard to envision near-term because three spending tailwinds stack: AI capex, large federal deficit spending, and corporate capex juiced by the 'one big beautiful bill' bonus depreciation tax write-offs.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: Recession bears may need to push timing out years; the risk is asset-price correction independent of an economic downturn.
insights.quote: it gets really hard to be able to see a recession happening anytime, you know, in the near future as long as this just tsunami of spending is going on

insights.claim: Recent macro data is firming — two jobs reports showing faster job creation, expanding industrial production, and ISM manufacturing at 54 (highest reading in years) — much of which Taggart ties to AI-driven GDP growth.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: Strong data reduces near-term Fed-easing odds and supports the 'no recession yet' case, but ties economic strength to the fragile AI cycle.
insights.quote: the ISM manufacturing index, that's up at 54 now. That's the highest reading in years

insights.claim: Lloyd compares AI capex to the dot-com era when IT capex as a share of GDP 'shot up like a rocket ship' then plummeted and stayed depressed for years even as the internet grew more indispensable — implying a similar capex cliff ahead.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Even with a real technology, capex is mean-reverting; the second-order hit to suppliers and GDP when AI spend normalizes is the bigger macro risk.
insights.quote: it shot up like a rocket ship not unlike what we're seeing right now. And then it plummeted like a rock and stayed down for years

insights.claim: Taggart argues stock prices are more dependent on AI spending than the spending is on stocks: with ~$1T of US AI spend projected this year and ~$1.2T thereafter, if actual spend comes in at only ~60% of projection, AI stocks have to reprice sharply downward.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: Gives a quantifiable downside trigger — a capex shortfall to ~60% of plan — to watch as the leading signal for an AI-stock de-rating.
insights.quote: If that only comes in at 60% of that, the stocks are going to have to repric down.

insights.claim: A wave of gargantuan IPOs/raises is hitting the market — SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI/ChatGPT, and a large Google raise (with Berkshire Hathaway reportedly taking a chunk) — which New Harbor reads as classic late-stage insiders cashing in at peak valuations.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host) / Mike Preston (guest)
insights.so_what: IPO surges cluster near relative market highs (2000, 2007); the supply wave is a sentiment flag to lower exposure and tighten hedges.
insights.quote: we've got anthropic coming up. Chat GPT. Um, Google just announced a massive raise and ... Berkshire Hathaway is is taking a chunk of that

insights.claim: SpaceX is set to IPO at ~$135/share for roughly a $2 trillion first-day valuation (~$75B raised, ~3x the next-largest IPO, Saudi Aramco), even as Morningstar reportedly argues fair value is about half that.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: A $2T day-one valuation that no prior mega-cap reached at IPO signals froth; the Morningstar haircut quantifies the overpricing risk.
insights.quote: SpaceX comes out with a two trillion market cap on its first day. None of these other guys did that.

insights.claim: Historically the median IPO underperforms badly — down ~10% one year out, ~40% three years out — so playing IPOs requires picking the few outlier winners that drag the average positive, especially dangerous near a market top.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Base rates argue against buying hyped IPOs like SpaceX at the offer; better to wait for a post-IPO valuation reset.
insights.quote: the median IPO is down 10% ... Three years out, the median IPO stocks down 40%

insights.claim: Taggart says that despite liking SpaceX as a company, he will not buy the IPO and plans to wait, expecting a high probability of acquiring it later at a materially better valuation than the IPO price.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: A 'wait for the post-IPO drawdown' stance on the year's marquee offering — a template for disciplined entry on hyped listings.
insights.quote: I don't think I'm going to buy it for the IPO. I think I'm going to buy and wait

insights.claim: New Harbor keeps equity exposure capped at roughly 48% — deliberately lower than peers — and accepts underperforming a vertical market for short periods because they judge this environment too extreme to be fully invested.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: A concrete late-cycle allocation template: stay ~50% equity and tolerate relative lag to protect against a blowoff-top reversal.
insights.quote: Our equity exposure has been roughly the same, right around 48%.

insights.claim: Rather than trading in and out of positions on whipsawing indicators, New Harbor adjusts option hedges; they recently sold a July S&P 500 7,000 put at a loss when their breadth indicators flipped bullish, ready to re-add similar protection if signals turn negative.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Shows a hedging-over-rotation approach to manage whipsaw; the dropped put means they are currently less hedged and more bullish-tilted.
insights.quote: We had a July S&P 500 7,000 put. We sold it. We took a loss on it, but that's the cost of insurance

insights.claim: New Harbor's system relies on a 'weight of the evidence' dashboard of old-school technicals — bullish percent indices (S&P, NASDAQ, NYSE), point-and-figure breadth, relative-strength sector analysis, and moving averages — rather than fundamental valuation, which they note 'hasn't mattered.'
insights.speaker: Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Their actionable signals are breadth/technical, not valuation; valuation defines long-term risk, breadth defines tactical positioning.
insights.quote: old school tools like point and figure charting that measure breath, bullish p bullish percents, relative strength analysis of sectors

insights.claim: Lloyd warns that a turn could be either a benign sector rotation (money leaving AI into laggard value names, as happened early in the year) or a true broad reset, and he sees no obvious catalyst for laggards like financials to suddenly outperform.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: The fork between rotation and reset matters for whether to hide in value or de-risk entirely; lack of a financials catalyst tilts toward reset risk.
insights.quote: if we start to see some of these sectors pick up and broaden out I think it could be more of a rotational event than a than a heavy reset of markets

insights.claim: If incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh signals patience — not hiking rates and betting the current inflationary impulse is transitory — Darius Dale and others think the market could bubble up further from here.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: A dovish Warsh is the macro fuel that could extend the blowoff; Fed commentary on the inflation impulse is the key catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: with Kevin Worsh coming in ... if Kevin Worsh indicates that he's not going to hike rates ... the market could then bubble up from there

insights.claim: Equity valuations are at historic extremes (CAPE, Buffett indicator) that have reliably predicted poor forward returns; Goldman Sachs and John Hussman now project negligible-to-zero, and in Hussman's case negative, annualized returns over the next decade.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host) / John Lloyd (guest)
insights.so_what: Even bulge-bracket firms now forecast a 'lost decade'; retirees relying on equity returns should plan around near-zero real returns.
insights.quote: investors really should expect negligible to like zero or in John Husman's case, even negative um annualized returns over the next ... decade

insights.claim: Lloyd argues many 'cheapest ever' relative-value charts (utilities or financials vs the S&P) are 'chart crimes' — a sector only looks cheap because it is being compared to an S&P that has never been more expensive, not because it is actually cheap.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Cautions against rotating into 'cheap' laggards as a safe haven — relative cheapness vs an extreme benchmark is not absolute value.
insights.quote: it's kind of like a a chart crime of sorts because ... you're comparing something to another thing that's never been more expensive

insights.claim: Negative-return 'lost decades' are not random Monte-Carlo coin flips but occur almost predictably from extreme starting valuations, so standard financial-planning tools using historical-average return assumptions systematically overstate retirees' odds of success.
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Planners and DIY investors should haircut expected returns for the next decade given today's valuations rather than trust default tool assumptions.
insights.quote: they don't just happen randomly. They happen almost predictably and reliably when we're at very extreme valuations like we are at now

insights.claim: Guardrail (flexible) spending — loosening in good market years and tightening in bad ones — dramatically raises retirement plan success odds versus a rigid inflation-adjusted fixed-withdrawal assumption, which counts running short by even $1 as a 'failure.'
insights.speaker: John Lloyd (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Adaptive withdrawal rules materially improve plan resilience to sequence-of-returns risk — especially valuable entering a likely low-return decade.
insights.quote: if you kind of be careful how much you spend and the pattern you spend given what happens in markets you can actually increase your odds of success quite dramatically

insights.claim: Energy stocks had a quiet early-2026 tear (helping drive the narrow S&P gains), boosted by oil prices rising on the prolonged Iran conflict and a Strait of Hormuz closure, but have been roughly flat over the past two months.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston / John Lloyd (guests, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Energy's leadership has stalled; with semis now doing the lifting, an energy re-acceleration would be one way breadth could broaden.
insights.quote: as oil started ... shooting up because of the the the straight of Hormuz closure um it got even more tailwind

insights.claim: Precious metals are consolidating near their correction-low levels with no change week-over-week — New Harbor frames the metals complex as a trade that currently requires patience after its earlier parabolic run.
insights.speaker: Mike Preston (guest, New Harbor Financial)
insights.so_what: Metals are in a holding pattern, not a fresh signal; position sizers should expect dead time rather than chase.
insights.quote: They're kind of floating along the their correction low levels. And so that's a trade that requires patience right now.

insights.claim: Bitcoin has broken through notable price-support levels, opening the question of how much further it could fall — flagged by Taggart as a developing risk to revisit.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host)
insights.so_what: A breakdown through support in Bitcoin can be an early risk-off / liquidity tell for speculative assets broadly; worth monitoring.
insights.quote: we actually have seen Bitcoin break through some some pretty notable price support levels
